coredata.dc:title: A water-soluble naphthalimide fluorescent probe for Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ based on inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Chuntian
Luo, Jiangxiong
Wang, Yijun
Ding, Ling
Liang, Qingxiang
Yang, Zhihui
Lu, Jihao
Wu, Aibin
coredata.dc:description: A probe 3 (2-ethoxy-N-(2-(2-(2-hydroxyethoxy)ethyl)-1,3-dioxo-2,3-dihydro-1H-benzo[de] isoquinolin-6-yl)benzamide) that could selectively respond to Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ was reported in this paper. The selectivity, pH titration, concentration titration, detection limit, time dependence, quenching constant and recognition mechanism of probe 3 for Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ were studied in CH3CN/HEPES buffer solution. The results showed that Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ could rapidly quench the fluorescence of probe 3 through the inner filter effect (IFE). The quenching kept constant after 30 s, and the quenching constants were 7.99 × 103 L.mol−1 and 4.13 × 103 L.mol−1, respectively. The detection limits of probe 3 for Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ were 1.15 μmol.L-1 and 1.95 μmol.L-1, respectively, which were lower than the maximum allowable concentrations in drinking water stipulated by EPA. The determination results of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ in water samples indicated that probe 3 could be used as a potential detection tool in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cr2O7 2-
Fe3+
Naphthalimide
Fluorescent probe
Inner filter effect

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and emission (λex = 300 nm, ex slit = 15 nm, em slit = 2.5 nm) spectra of probe 3 (CH3CN, 10 μmol.L-1).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of probe 3 (10 μmol·L-1, CH3CN: H2O = 1: 9) under different pH (λex = 350 nm, ex slit = 15 nm, em slit = 2.5 nm); (b) The curves between fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm and different pH values.

Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 350 nm, ex slit = 15 nm, em slit = 2.5 nm) of probe 3 in CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol.L-1, pH = 7.4) buffer solution upon addition of anions (S2O5 2-, CH3COO–, ClO-, Cr2O7 2-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, Br-, HCO3 –, H2PO4 -, Cl-, S2O3 2-, HSO3 -, ClO3 -, I-, SO4 2-, NO2 –).

(a) Fluorescence titration spectra of probe 3 (10 μmol.L-1) in CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol.L-1, pH = 7.4) buffer solution upon addition of Cr2O7 2-; (b) Curve relationship between fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm and different Cr2O7 2- concentrations.

Comparison of fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm when Cr2O7 2- and its interference ions (S2O5 2-, CH3COO–, ClO-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, Br-, HCO3 –, H2PO4 -, Cl-, S2O3 2-, HSO3 -, ClO3 -, I-, SO4 2-, NO2 –) of double concentration are added.

Fluorescence spectra (λex = 350 nm, ex slit = 15 nm, em slit = 2.5 nm) of probe 3 (10 μmol.L-1) in CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol.L-1, pH = 7.4) buffer solution upon addition of cations (Pb2+, Na+, Ba2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Mg2+).

(a) Fluorescence titration spectra of probe 3 (10 μmol.L-1) in CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol.L-1, pH = 7.4) buffer solution upon addition of Fe3+; (b) Curve relationship between fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm and different Fe3+ concentrations.

Comparison of fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm when Fe3+ and its interference ions (Pb2+, Na+, Ba2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Mg2+) of double concentration are added.
The absorption spectra of probe 3, Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+, and the fluorescence spectrum probe 3 in CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol.L-1, pH = 7.4) buffer solution.
Distinction of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ and the reversibility of probe 3 assisted by EDTA.
Reagents and conditions: a) 2-(2-aminoethoxy) ethanol, DMF, 150 ℃; b) 2-ethoxybenzoyl chloride, Et3N, CH3CN, 90 ℃.
Determination of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ in water samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A water-soluble naphthalimide fluorescent probe for Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical & Vehicle Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanical & Vehicle Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300392, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300392, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A water-soluble naphthalimide fluorescent probe, which could specific recognize Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ selectively and sensitively based on inner filter effect, was reported. The result of practical application in water samples indicated that the probe could be used as a potential tool for detecting Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+.

A probe 3 (2-ethoxy-N-(2-(2-(2-hydroxyethoxy)ethyl)-1,3-dioxo-2,3-dihydro-1H-benzo[de] isoquinolin-6-yl)benzamide) that could selectively respond to Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ was reported in this paper. The selectivity, pH titration, concentration titration, detection limit, time dependence, quenching constant and recognition mechanism of probe 3 for Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ were studied in CH3CN/HEPES buffer solution. The results showed that Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ could rapidly quench the fluorescence of probe 3 through the inner filter effect (IFE). The quenching kept constant after 30 s, and the quenching constants were 7.99 × 103 L.mol−1 and 4.13 × 103 L.mol−1, respectively. The detection limits of probe 3 for Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ were 1.15 μmol.L-1 and 1.95 μmol.L-1, respectively, which were lower than the maximum allowable concentrations in drinking water stipulated by EPA. The determination results of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ in water samples indicated that probe 3 could be used as a potential detection tool in practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cr2O7 2- is a heavy metal anion salt which is widely used in chrome plating, metallurgy, pigment, leather and anticorrosion, and has high toxicity, carcinogenicity, teratogenicity and bioaccumulation [1–6]. Cr2O7 2- is not only identified as a “A” carcinogen by the World Health Organization [7,8], but also listed as a priority contaminant by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and has set the maximum allowable concentration of Cr2O7 2- in drinking water at 1.92 μmol.L-1 [9–11]. In addition, Fe3+ is an essential trace element in organisms, and its deficiency or excess can lead to many health problems [12–14], such as osteoporosis, depression, organ failure, parkinson's disease, huntington's disease, alzheimer's disease, anemia, insomnia and hair loss [8,14–18]. Therefore, determination of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ content in the environment or in vivo is of great significance for life, health, environmental safety, medical diagnosis and treatment.

The existing ion detection methods for Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ include chromatography, mass spectrometry, electrochemical atomic absorption/emission spectrometry, graphite flame atomic absorption spectrometry, voltammetry, high performance liquid chromatography, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry, spectrophotometry and ion mobility spectroscopy [19–26]. However, most of these techniques are expensive, low sensitivity, time-consuming, complex pretreatment, and require skilled operators [5,17,27,28]. In contrast, fluorescent probe detection technique has the characteristics of high selectivity, high sensitivity, low cost, rapid response, visualization and biological imaging, so it is gaining favour gradually by many researchers [15,21,25,29–33]. At present, there are few reports about Cr2O7 2- fluorescent probes, mostly based on metal organic framework structure (MOFs). Although the preparation process of MOFs is simple, the preparation or detection process may cause metal ions such as Zn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Eu2+, In3+, Mn2+, Zr2+, Pb2+ to enter the environment, and usually require ultrasonic vibration or room temperature storage for a certain time [34–43]. Meanwhile, there are many reports on Fe3+ fluorescent probes, mainly on organic small molecules, MOFs and quantum dots [29,34,44–48]. However, considering the practical application of the reported Fe3+ fluorescent probes, researchers believe that they still remain to be developed and improved [15,19,21,24]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ fluorescent probes with convenient detection, sensitive response, safe use and strong practicability.

1,8-naphthalimide is the photosensitive part of photochemical sensor. It not only has a large rigid plane and conjugated π system, but also has the characteristics of bright colour and fluorescence, good photostability, high quantum yield, large Stokes shift, good photobleaching resistance and easy modification [49–51]. In this paper, we designed and synthesized a naphthalimide-based fluorescent probe 3 that responded to Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching. The selectivity, detection limit, quenching constant, time dependence, action mechanism as well as detection effect in real water samples of probe 3 in response to Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ were explored in CH3CN/HEPES solution. The results showed that probe 3 had good selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+, and could be used as a potential tool for the detection of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+.

All the analytical grade reagents and solvents were purchased from commercial sources and used as-received without further purification. An aqueous solution with a concentration of 0.01 mol.L-1 were prepared from chlorides or nitrates of metal ions (Pb2+, Na+, Ba2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Fe2+). An anionic aqueous solution with a concentration of 0.01 mol.L-1 (Cr2O7 2-, S2O5 2-, CH3COO–, ClO-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, Br-, HCO3 –, H2PO4 -, Cl-, S2O3 2-, HSO3 -, ClO3 -, I-, SO4 2-, NO2 –) were prepared from sodium or potassium salts; The concentration of ClO- was determined at 292 nm (ε = 365 L.mol−1.cm−1).

The preparation of compound 1 refered to the reported literature [52,53]. Acenaphthene was used as raw material, and compound 1 was obtained through nitration, oxidation and reduction reactions. 90.1 % yield. Orange solid, m.p. 284.1–285.2 ℃. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 8.92–8.00 (m, 3H), 7.74 (m, 3H), 6.87 (s, 1H) ppm. 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 162.50, 160.82, 154.40, 136.39, 133.51, 133.07, 131.18, 124.88 119.78, 118.77, 109.20, 102.71 ppm. MS (ESI) calcd for C12H6NO3 [M−H]- 212.0, found 212.0. Anal. calcd for C12H7NO3 C, 67.61; H, 3.31; N, 6.57; O, 22.51; found C, 67.60; H, 3.31; N, 6.58; O, 22.52.

Compound 1 (400 mg, 1.88 mmol) and DMF (2 mL) were taken to reflux at 150 ℃, and diethylene glycol amine (250 μL, 2.50 mmol) was added. After TCL monitoring showed that the raw materials was completed, the solution was cooled to room temperature and water was added to separate out the product. The residue was collected by suction filtration, and washed with water and absolute ethanol to obtain 581.3 mg of compound 2. 89.5 % yield. Orange solid, m.p. > 300 ℃. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 8.67–8.54 (m, 1H), 8.47–8.34 (m, 1H), 8.19 (dd, J = 8.3, 2.6 Hz, 1H), 7.70–7.57 (m, 1H), 7.45 (s, 2H), 6.89–6.75 (m, 1H), 4.57 (s, 1H), 4.20 (d, J = 6.3 Hz, 2H), 3.62 (t, J = 6.3 Hz, 2H), 3.47 (s, 4H) ppm. 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 164.30, 163.36, 153.24, 134.48, 131.53, 130.20, 129.83, 124.44, 122.15, 119.82, 108.65, 107.88, 72.55, 67.57, 60.66, 38.79 ppm. MS (ESI) calcd for C16H15N2O4 [M−H]- 299.1, found 299.2. Anal. calcd for C16H16N2O4 C, 63.99; H, 5.37; N, 9.33; O, 21.31; found C, 63.98; H, 5.35; N, 9.37; O, 21.32.

Compound 2 (78.9 mg, 0.2 mmol) and CH3CN (2 mL) were taken to reflux at 110 ℃, and furoyl chloride 100 μL and triethylamine 100 μL were added. After TCL monitoring showed that the raw materials was completed, column separation was carried out with CH2Cl2: CH3OH = 50: 1 as eluent to obtain 63.5 mg of probe 3. 70.9 % yield. Bright yellow solid, m.p. > 300 ℃. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 10.78 (s, 1H), 8.57–8.48 (m, 3H), 8.09 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 1H), 8.04 (dd, J = 1.7, 0.7 Hz, 1H), 7.88 (dd, J = 8.5, 7.3 Hz, 1H), 7.53 (dd, J = 3.5, 0.7 Hz, 1H), 6.79 (dd, J = 3.5, 1.7 Hz, 1H), 4.57 (s, 1H), 4.26 (t, J = 6.5 Hz, 2H), 3.67 (t, J = 6.5 Hz, 2H), 3.48 (s, 4H) ppm. 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ: 163.95, 163.47, 157.43, 147.45, 146.87, 140.08, 131.57, 131.57, 130.73, 128.78, 127.12, 126.47, 123.24, 122.67, 119.47, 116.33, 112.83, 72.59, 67.39, 60.66, 39.24 ppm. HRMS (ESI) calcd for C25H25N2O6 [M + H]+ 449.1713, found 449.1698. Anal. calcd for C25H24N2O6 C, 66.95; H, 5.39; N, 6.25; O, 21.40; found C, 66.95; H, 5.37; N, 6.27; O, 21.42.
The synthetic route of probe 3 was shown in Scheme 1 , and the detailed experimental process and characterization data were shown in the experimental part.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of probe 3 were obtained in CH3CN, as shown in Fig. 1 . The absorption peak of probe 3 at 378 nm was generated by the n-π* transition of the CO bond in amide, and the maximum fluorescence emission peak was at 452 nm. The test medium had an impact on the fluorescence properties of the probe. In this study, a water-miscible solvents were selected as the probe test medium (Figure S1). The results showed that probe 3 had the most sensitive fluorescence emission in CH3CN. Therefore, CH3CN was selected as the medium for the recognition performance of probe 3.

In order to eliminate the interference caused by pH in the identification process of analytes, the pH titration experiment of probe 3 were carried out in CH3CN: H2O = 1: 9, and the corresponding relationship between the fluorescence emission intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm and the pH value were established as shown in Fig. 2 . The results showed that pH had an effect on the fluorescence intensity of probe 3, and the fluorescence intensity dropped sharply when pH > 11. Therefore, it was necessary to study the ion recognition performance of probe 3 in a buffer system.
The recognition performances of probe 3 to analytes were studied in CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol·L-1, pH = 7.4) buffer solution and the excitation wavelength was at 350 nm.

The anions were added to the CH3CN: HEPES buffer solution (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol.L-1, pH = 7.4) of probe 3 with a concentration of 10 μmol·L-1, respectively. Under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the fluorescence spectra of probe 3 were shown in Fig. 3 . Obviously, the change of fluorescence intensity of probe 3 caused by other anions could be neglected, in addition to Cr2O7 2- quenching the fluorescence to a greater extent. Using quinine sulfate solution (φ = 0.54, 0.1 mol·L-1 H2SO4) as the standard, the relative fluorescence quantum yields in the absence and presence of Cr2O7 2- in the probe 3 were 0.22 and 0.04, respectively. Based on the effective fluorescence quenching by Cr2O7 2-, the fluorescence titration experiment were further carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 a, different concentrations of Cr2O7 2- were added to CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol·L-1, pH = 7.4) buffer solution of probe 3 with a concentration of 10 μmol.L-1, respectively. In order to quickly and clearly observe the change trend of the fluorescence intensity of the probe 3 with the concentration of Cr2O7 2-, the fluorescence intensity at 467 nm and the concentration of Cr2O7 2- were plotted as shown in Fig. 4b. It could be seen that with the increase of Cr2O7 2- concentration, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 and Cr2O7 2- concentration gradually deviated from linearity. According to existing literature reports, this phenomenon was caused by the energy transfer process or the self-absorption process [1,10,47,54]. However, the 0–60 μmol.L-1 Cr2O7 2- concentration had a good linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm to achieve quantitative detection (Figure S2), and the linear relationship was: y = 746.281 – 5.008x (R2 = 0.997).

By the Stern-Volmer equation I0/I = 1 + Ksv[M], the fluorescence quenching constant Ksv of Cr2O7 2- for probe 3 was calculated to be 7.99 × 103 L.mol−1. According to the formula D = 3σ/k, the detection limit D of probe 3 for Cr2O7 2- was 1.15 μmol.L-1 (σ = 1.91, k = 5.00). It was less than the maximum allowable concentration of Cr2O7 2- in water specified by EPA, which was 1.92 μmol.L-1. Therefore, probe 3 could be used as a fluorescent probe to detect Cr2O7 2- at a concentration of 1.15–60 μmol.L-1.

The anti-interference ability of probe 3 to Cr2O7 2- detection was obtained, by adding Cr2O7 2- and 15 interference ions with twice the amount of Cr2O7 2- into the CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1: 9, 10 mmol.L-1, pH = 7.4) buffer solution of probe 3 with a concentration of 10 μmol.L-1, as shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that probe 3 has strong selectivity and anti-interference when detecting Cr2O7 2-. At the same time, the time dependence of the response of probe 3 to Cr2O7 2- was tested, and the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm and the addition time of Cr2O7 2- were established (Figure S3). The results showed that the fluorescence of probe 3 were rapidly quenched by Cr2O7 2-, and the quenching kept stable within 30 s and remained constant at least 60 min.

Besides, we explored the cations recognition performance of probe 3 (Fig. 6 ). Obviously, only Fe3+ could cause the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 to be significantly weakened at 467 nm. The relative fluorescence quantum yield of the probe 3 decreased from 0.22 to 0.14 with the addition of Fe3+. Therefore, probe 3 could also be used as a Fe3+ selective fluorescence quenching probe.

The fluorescence titration spectra of probe 3 about Fe3+ and the variation trend of its fluorescence intensity at 467 nm with the concentration of Fe3+ ions were shown in Fig. 7 . Similar to the Cr2O7 2- titration, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 and Fe3+ concentration gradually deviated from linearity with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. However, the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm and Fe3+ concentration in 0–100 μmol.L-1 still had a good linear relationship to achieve quantitative detection (Figure S4). The linear relationship was: y = 807.410 – 2.942x (R2 = 0.993). The fluorescence quenching constant Ksv of Fe3+ to probe 3 was 4.13 × 103 L.mol−1, and the detection limit D was 1.95 μmol.L-1, which was lower than the Chinese drinking water standard (GB5749-2006) and the maximum allowable concentration of Fe3+ in drinking water stipulated by the US EPA 5.36 μmol.L-1. Therefore, probe 3 could be also used as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ in the detection concentration of 1.95–100 μmol.L-1. 18 cations were used to test the anti-interference of probe 3 in the detection of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 8 . The results show that probe 3 has good selectivity and anti-interference in the detecting Fe3+. In addition, the curve of the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 at 467 nm with the addition time of Fe3+ (Figure S5) indicated that the fluorescence of probe 3 could be rapidly quenched by Fe3+, and reached a stable state within 30 s, and remained constant for at least 60 min.

After adding Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+, the UV–vis absorption peak at 378 nm shifted blue and the intensity increased, and a wide weak absorption band appeared in the range of 400–510 nm. It was found that most of the existing Cr2O7 2- probes were based on fluorescence quenching caused by spectral overlap between them [1,3,5–8,10–12,34–38,40–43,46,55,56], and some Fe3+ probes also had similar spectral changes [5,7,8,10,26,34,37,38,42,43,46,55] through literature investigation. Ghasem Shiravand and Wudong Liu clearly pointed out that “the spectral overlap between the quencher absorption band and the excitation and/or emission band of the fluorescent probe could form an inner filter effect (IFE)”, and explained that the probe recognized Cr2O7 2- based on the IFE mechanism through the probe absorption, emission spectra and Cr2O7 2- absorption spectrum [6,56]. Therefore, we compared the UV–vis absorption spectra of probe 3, Cr2O7 2-, Fe3+ and the fluorescence spectrum of probe 3 as shown in Fig. 9 . Obviously, the fluorescence quenching between Cr2O7 2-, Fe3+ and probe 3 were due to the formation of IFE, not the structural damage of probe or the complexation between the probe and ions.

To evaluate the practical application ability of probe 3, we performed the detection of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ in water samples (Table 1 ). The data showed that the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the detected values of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ in water samples were less than 1.23 % and 3.16 %, respectively, indicating that probe 3 could be used as a potential detection tool for Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ in samples.

To distinguish the fluorescence quenching of probe 3 caused by Cr2O7 2- or Fe3+ and realize the reversible detection of probe 3, EDTA is used as a chelating agent or auxiliary agent for research, as shown in Fig. 10 . Obviously, the addition of EDTA does not affect the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 and the fluorescence intensity of probe 3 when detecting Cr2O7 2- or Fe3+. Therefore, EDTA cannot be used as a chelating agent or an auxiliary agent to achieve the detection and differentiation of Cr2O7 2- or Fe3+ by probe 3 and the reversible application of probe 3.

In summary, a novel naphthalimide Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ fluorescent probe 3 based on IFE recognition mechanism was designed and synthesized. In CH3CN: HEPES (v/v, 1:9, pH = 7.4) buffer solution, the fluorescence of probe 3 could be rapidly quenched by Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ with quenching constants of 7.99 × 103 L.mol−1 and 4.13 × 103 L.mol−1, respectively. The detection limits were 1.15 μmol.L-1 and 1.95 μmol.L-1, respectively, which were lower than the maximum allowable concentrations of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ in drinking water stipulated by EPA. Moreover, the detection results of water samples showed that the compound had potential practical application value as a highly selective and sensitive Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ fluorescent probe.

Chuntian Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration. Jiangxiong Luo: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Yijun Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ling Ding: Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation. Qingxiang Liang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation. Zhihui Yang: Resources, Data curation. Jihao Lu: Resources, Data curation. Aibin Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science and Technology Major Project of China (No. 2017ZX05049-003-007) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52274029).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aqueous-phase nanomolar detection of dichromate by a recyclable Cd(II) metal-organic framework
Cryst. Growth Des.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Gogoi, S. Biswas, Aqueous-Phase Nanomolar Detection of Dichromate by a Recyclable Cd(II) Metal-Organic Framework, Crystal Growth & Design, 21 (2021) 2680-2689.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mitochondria-targeted acridine-based dual-channel fluorescence chemosensor for detection of Sn4+ and Cr2O7 2- ions in water and its application in discriminative detection of cancer cells
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ravichandiran, D.S. Prabakaran, N. Maroli, A. R. Kim, B. H. Park, M. K. Han, T. Ramesh, S. Ponpandian, D. J. Yoo, Mitochondria-targeted acridine-based dual-channel fluorescence chemosensor for detection of Sn4+ and Cr2O72- ions in water and its application in discriminative detection of cancer cells, Journal of Hazardous Materials, 419 (2021) 126409.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A zinc(II) triazolate framework with luminescence response toward dichromate anion in aqueous solution
Inorg. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Y. Zou, L. Li, S. Y. You, S. W. Zhang, A zinc(II) triazolate framework with luminescence response toward dichromate anion in aqueous solution, Inorganica Chimica Acta, 498 (2019) 119126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly stable multi-response zirconium(IV) metal-organic frameworks for fluorescence sensing of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2- and organic small molecules
Chin. J. Org. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ma, L. Han, X. Zhang, Z. Hao, W. Yang, Y. Zhang, L. Wang, A Highly Stable Multi-response Zirconium(IV) Metal-Organic Frameworks for Fluorescence Sensing of Fe3+, Cr2O72- and Organic Small Molecules, Chinese Journal of Organic Chemistry, 40 (2020) 2938-2948.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bifunctional chemosensor based on a dye-encapsulated metal-organic framework for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cr2O7 2- and Fe3+ ions
Polyhedron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. A. Akram, J. Ye, G. Wang, L. Shi, Z. Liu, H. Lu, S. Zhang, G. Ning, Bifunctional chemosensor based on a dye-encapsulated metal-organic framework for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cr2O72- and Fe3+ ions, Polyhedron, 185 (2020) 114604.
